original_hypothesis.title: Shared Nonce Space Between permit() and transferWithAuthorization() Causes Cross-Function Invalidation
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: permit() and transferWithAuthorization() share the same _nonces mapping via _useNonce(). Executing one function consumes the nonce and permanently invalidates any pending signed authorization of the other function at the same nonce. A user who signs both a permit and a TWA at the same nonce loses whichever is executed second. Additionally, anyone holding a valid signed message of either type can execute it to consume the nonce, invalidating the other type.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: EIP-2612 (permit) and EIP-3009 (transferWithAuthorization) are designed with separate nonce domains. Merging them creates invalidation vectors that can cause failed transfers, missed payments, and user fund lockups when integrating with DeFi protocols.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Both permit() and transferWithAuthorization() call _useNonce(owner/holder) on the same _nonces mapping, consuming sequential nonces from a shared pool

preconditions: User signs both permit and TWA messages with the same nonce value
Both messages are distributed to different relayers or dApps

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _nonces[owner] is incremented by both permit() and transferWithAuthorization()
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Execute a permit to consume nonce N, invalidating a pending TWA at nonce N
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack: prevent user's transfer from executing, potentially causing missed payments or failed DeFi interactions

economic_questions: Do any DeFi integrations rely on both permit and TWA for EURF?
Can an attacker profit from preventing specific transfers?
What is the cost of nonce desync for users?
validation_requirements: Confirm shared nonce pool in code
Evaluate EIP-2612 and EIP-3009 compliance requirements

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 (only needs a previously obtained valid signed message)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; griefing causes failed transfers worth arbitrary amounts

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When victim has pending signed messages of both types
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could disrupt DeFi integrations relying on EURF meta-transactions

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who sign both permit and TWA messages
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Failed transfers, missed payment deadlines, stuck DeFi positions
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on value of invalidated authorizations

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm EIP-3009 specifies separate authorization nonces (bytes32, not sequential)

validation_result.mechanism: shared sequential nonce pool across permit and transferWithAuthorization
validation_result.impact_summary: permit() and transferWithAuthorization() share the same _nonces mapping and _useNonce() function, enabling cross-function nonce consumption that invalidates pending signed messages of the other type. This also deviates from EIP-3009 which specifies non-sequential bytes32 authorization IDs.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable contract defines a single _nonces mapping (line ~10) and a single _useNonce function (lines ~118-121) that both permit() and transferWithAuthorization() consume. When a user signs both a permit and a transferWithAuthorization at the same nonce value N, executing either one increments the nonce to N+1, permanently invalidating the other. This is a design defect on two levels: (1) EIP-3009 specifies that transferWithAuthorization should use unique bytes32 authorization IDs checked against a mapping(address => mapping(bytes32 => bool)), not sequential nonces shared with EIP-2612 permit; (2) even if sequential nonces were acceptable, sharing them between two functionally independent authorization mechanisms creates an invalidation vector that is both unexpected and exploitable for griefing. An attacker holding a valid signed permit can front-run a pending transferWithAuthorization (or vice versa) by executing the permit first, consuming the nonce and causing the TWA to revert.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The _nonces mapping is declared once at ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable.sol line ~10. Both permit() (line ~63 structHash construction) and transferWithAuthorization() (line ~86 structHash construction) call _useNonce() on this same mapping. There is no separate nonce domain, no bytes32 authorization state, and no mechanism to prevent cross-function invalidation. The code evidence directly and unambiguously confirms the shared nonce pool.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Single nonce counter shared by both permit() and transferWithAuthorization(). Incrementing via one function invalidates any pending signed message of the other function at the same nonce value.
permit() calls _useNonce(owner) consuming the shared nonce, which invalidates any pending transferWithAuthorization signed at the same nonce.
transferWithAuthorization() calls _useNonce(holder) consuming the same shared nonce, which invalidates any pending permit signed at the same nonce.

Single mapping used as nonce source for both permit and transferWithAuthorization. EIP-3009 specifies bytes32 nonce with separate authorizationState mapping, not sequential integers shared with EIP-2612.

calling_sequence: 1. User signs permit(owner, spender, value, nonce=N, deadline) off-chain
2. User also signs transferWithAuthorization(holder=owner, spender2, value2, nonce=N, deadline2) off-chain
3. Either message is submitted on-chain (by user, relayer, or attacker holding the signed message)
4. The executed function calls _useNonce(owner) which reads _nonces[owner]=N and sets _nonces[owner]=N+1
5. The other signed message now references nonce N, but _nonces[owner] is N+1
6. When the second message is submitted, _useNonce returns N+1, structHash differs from what was signed, ECDSA.recover returns wrong address, revert with InvalidSignature()
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Both permit() at ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable.sol:63 and transferWithAuthorization() at ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable.sol:86 call _useNonce() at ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable.sol:118-121 on the same _nonces mapping at ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable.sol:10. The nonce value is embedded in the EIP-712 structHash, so consuming a nonce for one function type makes any signed message of the other function type at that nonce cryptographically invalid (the recovered signer won't match).

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects the shared nonce design. The defect is inherent in the contract code.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is straightforward and realistic: (1) User signs a permit at nonce N and gives it to DApp A; (2) User signs a transferWithAuthorization at nonce N and gives it to relayer B; (3) Relayer B executes the TWA first, consuming nonce N; (4) DApp A tries to execute the permit with nonce N, which reverts because nonce is now N+1. Alternatively, an attacker who obtains any valid signed message of either type can execute it solely to consume the nonce and invalidate a pending message of the other type. The attack requires zero capital and only gas costs (~0.001 ETH). The preconditions (user signs both types at same nonce, messages distributed to different parties) are realistic in DeFi workflows where permit is used for approvals and TWA for direct transfers.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity denial of service. Users who integrate with protocols expecting both permit and TWA functionality will encounter unexpected reverts. Failed transferWithAuthorization can cause missed payments, failed DeFi position entries, or stuck funds in multi-step transactions. The non-standard EIP-3009 implementation (sequential nonces instead of bytes32 authorization IDs) also breaks compatibility with any tooling or protocol expecting standard EIP-3009 behavior. Additionally, this creates a griefing vector where anyone holding a valid signed message of either type can strategically invalidate the other.
recommended_mitigations: Implement separate nonce domains: use the existing _nonces for permit (EIP-2612 compliant) and add a separate mapping(address => mapping(bytes32 => bool)) authorizationState for transferWithAuthorization (EIP-3009 compliant with bytes32 authorization nonces)
If sequential nonces must be used for TWA, at minimum use a separate counter (e.g., _twaNonces) to prevent cross-function invalidation
Consider implementing cancellation functions (cancelAuthorization) as specified in EIP-3009 for the authorization-based nonce model
Add integration tests that verify permit and TWA can be independently executed without interference
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy EURFToken via proxy, initialize. 2. User signs a permit with nonce=0 (owner=user, spender=A, value=100, deadline=future). 3. User signs a TWA with nonce=0 (holder=user, spender=B, value=50, deadline=future). 4. Execute the permit on-chain - verify it succeeds and nonce advances to 1. 5. Execute the TWA on-chain with nonce=0 - verify it reverts with InvalidSignature. 6. Alternatively, execute TWA first and verify permit reverts. This proves cross-function invalidation.
uncertainties: The extent of real-world impact depends on whether integrating protocols actually use both permit and TWA for EURF simultaneously
Whether the development team intentionally chose shared nonces as a simplification or this was an oversight
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with interleaved permit and TWA submissions to quantify invalidation frequency
Test with DeFi protocol integrations (e.g., Uniswap permit2, payment streaming protocols) that may use both permit and TWA
Verify that existing off-chain signing tooling correctly handles the non-standard sequential nonce for TWA
